name: Sliding Window Rate Limiter
description: Write a class 'RateLimiter' in rate_limiter.py with __init__(self, max_requests: int, window_seconds: float) and a method allow(self, timestamp: float) -> bool. allow() returns True if a request at the given timestamp is permitted under a sliding window of window_seconds seconds (i.e., no more than max_requests calls to allow() may have returned True within any window_seconds-second interval ending at timestamp), and False otherwise. Timestamps are provided by the caller (do not call time.time() internally) and are not guaranteed to be strictly increasing across separate RateLimiter instances, but within a single instance's usage they arrive in non-decreasing order. Use only the standard library.
test_code:
from rate_limiter import RateLimiter


def test_allows_up_to_limit_within_window():
    rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_seconds=10.0)
    assert rl.allow(0.0) is True
    assert rl.allow(1.0) is True
    assert rl.allow(2.0) is True
    assert rl.allow(3.0) is False


def test_old_requests_expire_out_of_window():
    rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=2, window_seconds=5.0)
    assert rl.allow(0.0) is True
    assert rl.allow(1.0) is True
    assert rl.allow(2.0) is False
    assert rl.allow(6.0) is True


def test_exact_window_boundary_still_counts_the_old_entry():
    rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=1, window_seconds=5.0)
    assert rl.allow(0.0) is True
    assert rl.allow(5.0) is False
    assert rl.allow(5.0001) is True


def test_zero_capacity_never_allows():
    rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=0, window_seconds=10.0)
    assert rl.allow(0.0) is False
    assert rl.allow(100.0) is False

name: Stable Priority Queue
description: Write a class 'StablePriorityQueue' in priority_queue.py with methods push(self, priority: int, item) and pop(self) -> object. Lower priority values are popped first. When two items have equal priority, they MUST be returned in FIFO order relative to each other (insertion order), even though Python's heapq does not guarantee this on its own. pop() on an empty queue must raise IndexError with a message containing the word 'empty'. Use only the standard library (heapq is allowed).
test_code:
import pytest
from priority_queue import StablePriorityQueue


def test_pops_lowest_priority_first():
    q = StablePriorityQueue()
    q.push(5, "low")
    q.push(1, "high")
    q.push(3, "mid")
    assert q.pop() == "high"
    assert q.pop() == "mid"
    assert q.pop() == "low"


def test_equal_priority_is_fifo():
    q = StablePriorityQueue()
    q.push(1, "first")
    q.push(1, "second")
    q.push(1, "third")
    assert q.pop() == "first"
    assert q.pop() == "second"
    assert q.pop() == "third"


def test_pop_empty_raises_index_error():
    q = StablePriorityQueue()
    with pytest.raises(IndexError, match="empty"):
        q.pop()


def test_interleaved_push_pop():
    q = StablePriorityQueue()
    q.push(2, "a")
    q.push(1, "b")
    assert q.pop() == "b"
    q.push(1, "c")
    assert q.pop() == "c"
    assert q.pop() == "a"

name: Debounce Decorator
description: Write a decorator factory 'debounce(wait_seconds: float, clock=None)' in debounce.py. When applied to a function f, the decorated function should only actually call f if at least wait_seconds have elapsed since the last call to f actually happened (as measured by the clock callable, which returns the current time in seconds; if clock is None, use time.monotonic). Calls made before wait_seconds have elapsed since the last actual call must be suppressed (return None) without calling f. The decorated function must work correctly across multiple independent decorated functions (state must not leak between different decorated functions). Use only the standard library.

name: Directed Graph Cycle Detection
description: Write a function 'has_cycle(graph: dict[str, list[str]]) -> bool' in graph_cycle.py that returns True if the given directed graph (adjacency list, mapping node name to a list of nodes it points to) contains a cycle, and False otherwise. The graph may contain nodes referenced only as targets (not as keys) and self-loops (a node pointing to itself count as a cycle). An empty graph has no cycle. Use only the standard library.
